food_subgroup: Spiced fried potato dish with condiments

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry for Batata Harra exists; closest_parent 'potato food product' (FOODON_03301710) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Potato products and dishes) is the closest parent category; no specific code for spiced Lebanese fried potato dish exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept specifically represents Batata Harra; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

allergens: garlic sauce may contain egg (mayonnaise base)
ketchup may contain traces of gluten

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Batata Harra is approximately 250 g, comprising spiced fried potatoes with coleslaw salad, pickles, garlic sauce, and ketchup.

nutrient_source.title: Potatoes, french fried, all types, salt added in processing, frozen, as purchased (USDA SR Legacy, NDB 11400 / FDC ID 170697) adapted with spice and condiment additions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core potato-fry macros drawn from USDA SR Legacy fried potato entry (FDC 170697). Values adjusted upward for sodium and fat to account for garlic sauce (mayonnaise-based), pickles, and ketchup components described in the dish. Dietary fibre and sugar adjusted to reflect mixed-dish composition. This is a composite estimate; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — fried potato / french fries analogous entry
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Batata Harra. GI of 65 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried potatoes / french fries (GI range 63–70, mean ~65). Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (65 × 25.5 × 2.50) / 100 = 41.4 g. The addition of garlic sauce and pickles does not materially alter the GI of the potato base.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from pickles, garlic sauce, and ketchup
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Batata Harra due to its elevated sodium content (approximately 380 mg per 100 g, ~950 mg per serving), primarily contributed by pickles, garlic sauce, and ketchup; portion reduction and requesting condiments on the side are recommended strategies.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from fried potato base with moderate GI of 65
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Batata Harra cautiously; a standard 250 g serving delivers an estimated glycemic load of approximately 41 g, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation, and portion size should be strictly limited to no more than 100–120 g per meal occasion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat and sodium from frying oil and condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Batata Harra presents moderate concern due to its fat content from deep-frying (approximately 9.5 g fat per 100 g) and high sodium from condiments; choosing a smaller portion and requesting reduced condiments can help manage saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Batata Harra (Arabic: بطاطا حارة, meaning 'spicy potatoes') is a popular Lebanese and Levantine dish consisting of fried or sautéed potato cubes seasoned with garlic, chilli, fresh coriander, lemon juice, and olive oil. The name literally translates to 'hot potatoes' in Arabic. In this Singapore restaurant version served at Jewel of India (Boat Quay), the dish is presented with coleslaw salad, pickles, garlic sauce, and ketchup, reflecting a fusion adaptation. The potatoes are typically deep-fried until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining fluffy inside, then tossed with the spice mixture. The flavour profile is bold, garlicky, tangy, and moderately spicy. Nutritionally, the dish is carbohydrate-dominant with moderate fat from frying, and elevated sodium from the condiment accompaniments. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium GI) based on the fried potato base, with a high glycemic load per serving (~41 g GL for 250 g). Clinically, the dish warrants caution for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its high glycemic load, and for those with hypertension due to its sodium content from pickles and sauces. It is suitable for vegetarians and is halal-friendly. Batata Harra is commonly served as a mezze side dish or appetiser in Lebanese cuisine and is widely popular across the Middle East and in Lebanese diaspora restaurants globally.

confidence_reason: No exact match for Batata Harra found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried potato data (FDC 170697) with composite adjustments for condiments. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous fried potato entry. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and the restaurant-specific variation in condiment quantities.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried potato base (FDC 170697) with upward sodium and fat adjustments for garlic sauce, pickles, and ketchup. A dedicated laboratory analysis of this specific dish would improve confidence to high. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients in the potato base; confirmed zero for the core potato component per USDA FoodData Central. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for fried potato products. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database fried potato entry; no direct GI measurement for Batata Harra exists in the peer-reviewed literature. 5. The cuisine field reflects the dish's Lebanese/Middle Eastern origin rather than the serving restaurant's Indian cuisine category. 6. The restaurant listed (Jewel of India, Boat Quay) serves this as a cross-cuisine menu item. 7. Serving size of 250 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant plate; actual portion may vary. 8. Vegan flag is false because garlic sauce typically contains egg-based mayonnaise; vegetarian flag is true.
